python.explanation: 15 and 6 are numbers, so + adds them. The result 21 is printed.

java.explanation: Both operands are ints, so + performs addition: 15 + 6 = 21.

javascript.explanation: Both values are numbers, so + adds them, giving 21.

cpp.explanation: 15 + 6 is evaluated as integer addition before it reaches cout, so it prints 21.

choices: Prints the literal text: Hello, name
Prints a greeting using the value stored in name
Causes an error because name is undefined
Prints Hello, on its own line, then Ada on the next
python.explanation: name holds "Ada", and print joins its arguments with a space, so it prints: Hello, Ada.

choices: Prints the literal text: Hello, name
Prints a greeting using the value stored in name
Causes an error because name is not a String
Prints Hello, and Ada on separate lines
java.explanation: + concatenates the literal "Hello, " with the value of name, printing: Hello, Ada.

choices: Prints the literal text: Hello, name
Prints a greeting using the value stored in name
Causes an error because name is a reserved word
Prints Hello, and Ada on separate lines
javascript.explanation: The + operator concatenates the string with the value of name, logging: Hello, Ada.

choices: Prints the literal text: Hello, name
Prints a greeting using the value stored in name
Causes an error because name is uninitialized
Prints Hello, and Ada on separate lines
cpp.explanation: Chained << outputs the literal then the value of name, printing: Hello, Ada.

python.prompt: Type exactly what this code outputs:
python.explanation: Both operands are strings, so + joins (concatenates) them into CS101.

java.prompt: Type exactly what this code outputs:
java.explanation: With String operands, + concatenates, producing CS101.

javascript.prompt: Type exactly what this code outputs:
javascript.explanation: When a string is on either side of +, JavaScript concatenates, giving CS101.

cpp.prompt: Type exactly what this program outputs:

python.explanation: // is floor (integer) division in Python, so 7 // 2 is 3 (the .5 is dropped).

java.explanation: Dividing two ints truncates toward zero in Java, so 7 / 2 is 3.

javascript.explanation: 7 / 2 is 3.5, and Math.floor rounds down to 3.

cpp.explanation: Both operands are ints, so C++ does integer division: 7 / 2 is 3.

python.prompt: Write a single statement that prints the word Hello (exactly).
python.explanation: Python prints with the print() function: print("Hello"). Single or double quotes both work.

java.prompt: Write a single statement that prints the word Hello on its own line.
java.explanation: Java prints a line with System.out.println("Hello"); â€” note the semicolon and double quotes.

javascript.prompt: Write a single statement that logs the word Hello to the console.
javascript.explanation: JavaScript logs with console.log("Hello"). Single, double, or backtick quotes all work.

cpp.prompt: Write a statement that prints the word Hello using cout.
cpp.explanation: C++ prints with cout << "Hello"; using the << stream operator (needs #include <iostream>).

python.prompt: Why does this code stop before it can run?
choices: The list is missing a closing bracket ]
python.explanation: The opening bracket starts a list, but no closing bracket ends it. Python cannot finish parsing the assignment. Matching each opening delimiter with its closing partner is a useful first syntax check.

java.prompt: Why does this code fail to compile?
choices: The array initializer needs a closing brace } before the semicolon
java.explanation: The opening brace begins the array initializer, so a closing brace must end it before the semicolon. The compiler cannot determine where the initializer ends.

javascript.prompt: Why does this code stop with a syntax error?
choices: The array is missing a closing bracket ]
javascript.explanation: The opening bracket starts the array, but the semicolon appears before a closing bracket. Add ] after 9 so JavaScript can finish parsing the array.

cpp.prompt: Why does this code fail to compile?
choices: The initializer needs a closing brace } before the semicolon
cout cannot print an array element
cpp.explanation: The opening brace begins the initializer list and needs a matching closing brace. Add } after 9 before ending the declaration with a semicolon.

python.explanation: A line beginning with # is a comment, so Python ignores it. Only the first and third lines run, printing 2 and then 4.

java.explanation: Everything after // on that line is a comment. The commented call is ignored, so the program prints 2 and then 4.

javascript.explanation: The // marks the rest of its line as a comment. JavaScript skips that line and logs 2 followed by 4.

cpp.explanation: The // comments out the second output statement. The compiler ignores it, leaving the statements that print 2 and 4.

choices: Python is case-sensitive, so Print is not print
python.explanation: Python treats uppercase and lowercase letters as different. The built-in function is named print with a lowercase p, so Print is an unknown name.

java.prompt: Why does this fail to compile?
choices: Java is case-sensitive, so system must be System
java.explanation: Java is case-sensitive. System is a class name with an uppercase S, while system refers to a different and undeclared name.

choices: JavaScript is case-sensitive, so Console must be console
javascript.explanation: The browser or runtime provides console with a lowercase c. Console is a different name and is not defined.

cpp.prompt: Why does this fail to compile?
choices: C++ is case-sensitive, so Cout must be cout
cpp.explanation: C++ treats Cout and cout as different identifiers. The standard output stream is cout with a lowercase c.

python.prompt: Type the two output lines, with a line break between them.
python.explanation: Inside a string, \n represents a newline character. The print call outputs A, moves to the next line, and then outputs B.

java.prompt: Type the two output lines, with a line break between them.
java.explanation: The escape sequence \n inserts a newline inside the String. println adds its own final line ending after B.

javascript.prompt: Type the two output lines, with a line break between them.
javascript.explanation: The \n escape becomes a newline character, so A and B appear on separate lines.

cpp.prompt: Type the two output lines, with a line break between them.
cpp.explanation: The \n escape tells the output stream to move to a new line between A and B.

questions.title: Mark the body of a decision

python.explanation: A Python if header ends with a colon. The indented line after it becomes the body that runs when the condition is true.

java.explanation: Java requires the if condition inside parentheses. The corrected header is if (score >= 70) followed by the block.

choices: A semicolon between if and score
javascript.explanation: JavaScript requires parentheses around an if condition. Write if (score >= 70) before the opening brace.

cpp.explanation: C++ requires the condition inside parentheses. Write if (score >= 70) and keep the statements inside braces.
